request: We need an app for staff to check attendees in at the venue entrance on the event day.

problem.value: Control and record physical entry to the event, replacing an ad-hoc/manual check at the door with a device-based flow.
problem.evidence: "app for staff to check attendees in at the venue entrance on the event day"

current_process.evidence: No mention of how entrance control is done today (paper list, spreadsheet, prior tool).

success_metrics.evidence: No stated success measure (speed, accuracy, no queue, no duplicate entries).

actors.value: On-site staff/hostess is the confirmed operator; event manager, security and possibly exhibitors likely involved but unconfirmed.

business_objects.value: Attendee, Registration and Check-in event implied by the check-in flow; Zone/Session and Category not mentioned.
business_objects.evidence: Implicit from "check attendees in at the venue entrance"

business_rules.value: Unclear what counts as a valid check-in (single entrance only vs multiple zones), and what triggers a block vs a warning.

workflow.value: A minimal scan then validate then record flow is implied, but states (duplicate, re-entry, per-zone) are undefined.

integrations.value: No mention of sync with a registration source, live dashboard, or multi-device data sharing.

permissions.value: Only 'staff' named; no mention of security overrides, manager visibility, or who can manually register a walk-in.

config_vs_custom.value: Unknown whether this is a one-off build for this event or the first instance of a reusable check-in kit for future events, which changes the data model and cost significantly.
config_vs_custom.evidence: Not addressed; flagged as a recurring blind spot in this domain.

constraints.value: The event date is fixed and immovable (explicit), but venue connectivity, the single biggest architecture driver, is unstated.
constraints.evidence: "on the event day" fixes the deadline; nothing said about network conditions.

edge_cases.value: Duplicate scans, re-entry after stepping out, multi-zone access, and offline fallback are all unaddressed.

reporting.value: Post-event attendance data (who came, when, where) is typically the client's real deliverable, not stated here.
reporting.evidence: Not addressed; flagged in context as a hard deliverable, not an afterthought.

risks.value: Fixed date means no second chance if the check-in path fails on the day; rehearsal and manual fallback not yet scoped.
risks.evidence: Inferred from the fixed "event day" constraint.

questions.q: Is this a one-off app for this specific event, or should it be built as the first instance of a reusable check-in kit for future events?
questions.why: High impact, zero information: this single answer reshapes the data model, configurability and cost of everything else, and clients rarely state it unprompted.

questions.q: Will the venue have reliable wifi/4G at the entrance, or must the app work fully offline (local attendee snapshot plus later sync)?
questions.why: High impact, unaddressed: offline vs online-only is an architecture decision, not a setting, and it drives error handling, UX and cost.

questions.q: When the same attendee is scanned twice, what should happen: block as duplicate, allow as re-entry, or record as a separate zone/session visit?
questions.why: High impact, undefined: these are three different data models, and the naive build (silent success) breaks first when multiple entrances scan concurrently.

questions.q: What should happen when a name isn't found on the list (misspelling, walk-in, late registration) or a QR code fails to scan?
questions.why: High impact, unaddressed: manual name search and on-the-spot registration are a meaningful share of real arrivals, not a rare exception, and change the required UI paths.

questions.q: After the event, what attendance data does the client need (who came, when, which zone) and in what format?
questions.why: High impact, unaddressed: attendance data is often the real deliverable for the client and their sponsors, not a byproduct of check-in.

summary.objective: Give on-site staff a device-based way to identify and record attendee arrivals at the venue entrance on the day of the event, replacing manual or paper-based entry control.
summary.scope: Likely includes: an attendee/registration list, a scan or search-based identification flow, a check-in record per attendee (possibly per zone/session), basic staff permissions, and a post-event attendance export. Whether it works offline, whether the same attendee can check in more than once, and whether this is reusable across future events are all still open.
assumptions: The primary operator is on-site staff/hostess, not the event manager or security, though their roles are unconfirmed.
A basic scan-then-record workflow is expected, but duplicate/re-entry/multi-zone handling is undefined.
Attendee, Registration and Check-in are the core objects; Zone/Session and Category are not yet confirmed as in scope.
summary.blind_spot: The client has framed this as a simple door-scanning app, but the two decisions that most determine cost and architecture, offline capability at the venue and whether this is a one-off build versus a reusable event kit, are both unstated. Getting either wrong after the fact is expensive because the event date cannot move and there is no second chance to fix it on the day.

problem.value: Reduce entrance queues and produce an accurate arrival record; the stated ask ('check attendees in') is the mechanism, not the underlying need (speed + accuracy + no double counting).
problem.evidence: "staff to check attendees in at the venue entrance on the event day"

actors.value: Staff/hostess as primary operator; likely also an event manager monitoring live numbers and possibly security at controlled zones.

business_objects.value: Attendee, Registration/expected list, Check-in event, possibly Zone/Session if access is segmented.
business_objects.evidence: implied by "check attendees in at the venue entrance"

constraints.value: Fixed, immovable event date implies no slippage; venue connectivity and device availability are likely unstated constraints.

reporting.evidence: Attendance data is typically the post-event deliverable (who came, when, where) for invoicing/reporting to client or sponsors — treated as high impact even though not mentioned.

risks.value: Primary risk is failure at the door on the day itself — no second chance, so manual fallback and rehearsal are likely required.
risks.evidence: fixed event date + entrance check-in is the most visible, least forgiving part of the build

questions.q: Is this app being built for this single event only, or is it meant to be reused as a configurable kit for future events?
questions.why: information_value = high uncertainty (empty) × high impact: this changes the data model (single-event vs multi-event), configuration approach, and cost basis for the whole build — yet it's almost never stated by the client.

questions.q: Will staff devices need to work without reliable network at the venue (offline scanning with later sync), or can we assume solid connectivity throughout?
questions.why: information_value = high uncertainty (empty) × high impact: offline vs online is an architecture decision (local snapshot, sync, conflict handling) that can't be retrofitted — and clients almost always assume it without saying so.

questions.q: What exactly should happen if the same attendee is scanned twice — is it always a duplicate to block, a re-entry to allow, or could it mean access to a different zone/session we need to track separately?
questions.why: information_value = high uncertainty (empty) × high impact: this single rule determines the data model for check-in events and the concurrency behavior across multiple entrance devices.

questions.q: What happens when a scan fails (no badge, unreadable QR, name not on the list, walk-in)? Do staff need a manual name-search and on-the-spot registration path?
questions.why: information_value = high uncertainty (empty) × high impact: identification failure is a routine share of real arrivals, not a rare edge case, and its absence causes queues — the visible failure mode the client fears.

questions.q: After the event, what attendance data does the client (or their sponsors) need — who came, when, at which zone — and in what format/export?
questions.why: information_value = high uncertainty (empty) × high impact: attendance data is typically the actual deliverable driving invoicing/lead follow-up, yet the request only mentions the check-in mechanism, not its output.

questions.q: Roughly how many attendees and how many simultaneous entrance points/devices are expected, and how many seconds per person can the door tolerate at peak?
questions.why: information_value = medium-high uncertainty (inferred only) × high impact: throughput at peak is a functional spec that decides UI simplicity, device count and concurrency handling — not a nice-to-have detail.

summary.objective: Give on-site staff a fast, reliable way to identify and check in attendees at the venue entrance on the day of the event, producing an accurate attendance record.
summary.scope: Likely includes: an expected-attendee list (imported, editable up to and during the event), a scanning/search-based identification flow for staff, a check-in action with a defined outcome for duplicates/re-entries/zone access, and a post-event attendance export. Whether it must work offline, support multiple simultaneous entrances, and be reusable across future events materially changes the scope and is currently unknown.
assumptions: The primary actor is on-site staff/hostess acting as the operator, not the attendee themselves.
The event has a fixed, non-negotiable date, implying no possibility of post-launch fixes for the check-in flow.
Attendance data will be needed after the event as a deliverable (who came, when), even though not explicitly requested.
Venue connectivity cannot be assumed reliable and may require an offline-capable design.
The attendee list is not frozen and will need on-site edits (walk-ins, substitutions, corrections).
summary.blind_spot: The client asked for 'check attendees in' as if it were a single simple action, but real-world entrance check-in hides several binary decisions that change the entire build: offline vs. online architecture, duplicate vs. re-entry vs. multi-zone semantics, and whether this is a one-off app or the first instance of a reusable event kit. None of these were stated, and each one flips the technical approach rather than just adding a feature.

problem.value: Avoid entrance bottlenecks and give the client an accurate record of who actually showed up, replacing a manual/paper check against a guest list.
problem.evidence: "app for staff to check attendees in at the venue entrance" — the stated action (check-in) implies an underlying need for speed and accuracy at the door, not just a scan button.

current_process.evidence: Not stated how attendees are currently identified/tracked (paper list, spreadsheet, another registration tool).

success_metrics.value: Likely: no queue at peak arrival, accurate post-event attendance report.
success_metrics.evidence: Not stated; inferred from domain norm that throughput and attendance accuracy are the usual success criteria.

actors.value: On-site staff/hostess at entrance (explicit). Likely also: event manager (monitoring), security (access control), possibly exhibitor staff for zone-level check-in.
actors.evidence: "staff to check attendees in" — staff explicitly named as operator; other roles inferred from context.

business_objects.value: Attendee, Registration/expected-attendee list, Check-in event, possibly Zone/Session, Category.
business_objects.evidence: "check attendees in" implies an attendee list and a check-in record; zones/categories inferred from context, not stated.

business_rules.evidence: Not stated whether a second scan means duplicate, re-entry, or a different zone/session — a core semantic decision.

workflow.value: Presumed: attendee arrives → identified (scan/search) → checked in → recorded; possibly gated by zone/session and category.
workflow.evidence: Only the single action "check in" is described; the full lifecycle (identification, exceptions, multi-zone) is unstated.

integrations.value: Likely needs sync with a registration/import source and possibly a live dashboard; notification needs unstated.
integrations.evidence: Not mentioned; inferred from event-ops norm that attendee data originates elsewhere.

permissions.evidence: Not stated who can check in vs. view attendance vs. edit the list (staff vs. manager vs. security).

config_vs_custom.evidence: Not stated whether this is a one-off build for a single event or the first instance of a reusable check-in kit — this is flagged in the context as the decision that changes almost everything downstream.

constraints.value: Fixed, non-movable event date; venue connectivity likely imperfect; device/hardware TBD.
constraints.evidence: Implicit in "check-in at the venue entrance on the event day" — a real, one-shot, on-site operation.

edge_cases.evidence: Not addressed: offline connectivity, duplicate scans, concurrent entrances, walk-ins/unregistered attendees, name mismatches.

reporting.value: Likely need: post-event attendance export (who came, when, where).
reporting.evidence: Not stated, but context flags attendance data as a standard client deliverable — raised above the module's low baseline.

risks.value: Primary risk: check-in path fails live at the door in front of the client with no second chance; needs manual fallback and on-site rehearsal.
risks.evidence: Inferred from the event-ops domain note that the check-in flow is the least forgiving part of the build.

questions.q: Is this a one-off build for this single event, or the first instance of a check-in tool you'll reuse across future events?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: empty × high — this single answer changes the data model, configurability and cost of almost everything else; it's the blind spot named explicitly in the domain.

questions.q: Does the venue have reliable connectivity, or should the app work offline with check-ins syncing later?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: empty × high — offline vs. online-only is an architecture decision, not a setting, and is almost never stated by the client even though it drives the whole build.

questions.q: What should happen if the same attendee is scanned twice — is it always a duplicate to block, or can it mean a legitimate re-entry or access to a different zone/session?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: empty × high — this determines the check-in data model (one boolean vs. an event log) and the door-side UX for staff.

questions.q: Where does the attendee list come from today (registration tool, spreadsheet, nothing), and how will it reach the check-in app — one-time import or a live feed?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: empty × high — determines integration needs, freshness of the offline snapshot, and how late-arriving registrations/walk-ins are handled.

questions.q: Who besides the entrance staff needs access — can an event manager monitor live arrivals, and does security need a separate view or rights?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: empty × high — permission needs are typically forgotten until acceptance testing per the domain notes, and multiple entrances/roles change the access model.

summary.objective: Give on-site staff a fast, reliable way to identify and check attendees in at the venue entrance on the event day, producing an accurate attendance record.
summary.scope: Likely covers: attendee identification (scan and/or manual search), a check-in action recorded per attendee (with handling for duplicates, re-entry or multi-zone access), a staff-facing interface usable without training, and a resulting attendance export. Scope is highly sensitive to whether the venue has reliable connectivity and whether this is a one-off or reusable build.
assumptions: The underlying goal is avoiding entrance bottlenecks and producing an accurate attendance record, not just a scan action (inferred).
Staff are the primary operators, with an event manager and possibly security as secondary roles (inferred).
The attendee list, categories and zones exist as business objects even though only 'attendees' was mentioned (inferred).
The event date is fixed and venue connectivity may be imperfect (inferred from domain norms, not stated).
summary.blind_spot: The client asked for a check-in app but didn't say whether it must work offline, whether a second scan means a blocked duplicate or a valid re-entry/zone change, and whether this is a throwaway build or a reusable kit — each of these silently changes the architecture, not just a feature list.
